description: Enumerations for beat types, scene functions, dramatic polarity, event types, want outcomes, causal roles, and stakes domains.

beat_type.description: The story-structural beat category — where this scene falls in the macro narrative arc.

values.definition: First scene: establishes the before-state of the story world and protagonist.
values.example_usage: Snyder beat 1.

values.definition: World-as-it-is before the story disrupts it; establishes normalcy.
values.example_usage: Scenes immediately after opening that confirm the established world.

values.definition: The event that irreversibly disrupts the status quo and launches the central problem.
values.example_usage: The call to adventure; the murder; the discovery.

values.definition: The protagonist is explicitly invited or confronted with the possibility of the story's quest.
values.example_usage: Hero is asked to undertake a mission; invitation arrives.

values.definition: The protagonist hesitates or refuses the call, showing the stakes of crossing the threshold.
values.example_usage: The hero finds reasons to stay; initial resistance.

values.definition: The protagonist commits irreversibly to the main action; no turning back.
values.example_usage: End of Act 1 in three-act structure; the point of no return.

values.definition: Escalating complications as the protagonist pursues their want against opposition.
values.example_usage: Standard mid-story scenes.

values.definition: A false victory, false defeat, or revelation that shifts the stakes and raises the question.
values.example_usage: Structural midpoint; often a revelation or reversal.

values.definition: The protagonist's lowest point; all seems lost; the interior crisis before the climax.
values.example_usage: All Is Lost / Dark Night of the Soul beat.

values.definition: The highest-tension confrontation where the central question is definitively answered.
values.example_usage: Final battle; decisive confrontation; the point of maximum tension.

values.definition: Immediate aftermath of climax; the old question is settled and the new world is visible.
values.example_usage: The dénouement begins.

values.definition: Final restoration of story world; loose ends resolved; the after-state established.
values.example_usage: Epilogue; final scenes after resolution.

values.definition: A scene that occurs outside the main timeline and depicts earlier story-world events.
values.example_usage: A memory scene; backstory dramatized.

values.definition: A scene that occurs outside the main timeline and depicts later story-world events.
values.example_usage: A prologue from the end; anticipatory glimpse.

values.definition: A scene that pauses the main narrative thrust; may develop character, theme, or setting without advancing the plot.
values.example_usage: A quiet scene between two action beats; a thematic pause.

scene_function.description: The primary dramatic purpose of the scene (what it accomplishes for the story).

values.definition: Establishes or reveals a fact that changes the reader's or a character's understanding.
values.example_usage: The detective reveals the murderer; the character discovers the truth.

values.definition: Two or more characters come into direct conflict of will, value, or desire.
values.example_usage: A debate, fight, argument, or face-to-face opposition.

values.definition: A character makes a significant choice that shapes the plot and reveals character.
values.example_usage: The protagonist chooses to go or stay; commits to a course of action.

values.definition: The relationship between two characters changes fundamentally.
values.example_usage: A friendship breaks; a romance begins; trust is extended or withdrawn.

values.definition: Establishes or deepens the story world without primarily advancing plot.
values.example_usage: A scene showing the rules of a society, magic system, or environment.

values.definition: The protagonist's plan or goal suffers a significant failure or reversal.
values.example_usage: The plan fails; help is withdrawn; an ally turns enemy.

values.definition: A character, the reader, or both learn something new that wasn't previously accessible.
values.example_usage: A secret discovered; a document found; a connection made.

values.definition: Movement from one scene location, emotional state, or narrative position to another.
values.example_usage: Travel scenes; transitions between major sequences.

scene_polarity.description: The emotional/dramatic charge of the scene — whether it moves the protagonist's circumstances in a positive or negative direction on one or more axes.

values.definition: Scene begins well for the protagonist and ends better.
Scene begins well and ends badly — a reversal downward.
Scene begins badly and ends better — a reversal upward.
Scene begins badly and ends worse — deepening of trouble.
Scene begins at an established baseline and ends on a positive note.
Scene begins at baseline and ends badly.
The shift is uncertain, contested, or depends on whose perspective is used.
The shift results from a revelation that reframes what has already happened.
External circumstances improve while internal state worsens (or vice versa).
External circumstances worsen while the character grows, resolves, or achieves clarity.
Two reversals occur within the scene: positive → negative → positive or inverse.
Technically positive outcome that carries a hidden negative cost or implication.
Technically negative outcome that carries a hidden positive implication.
An expected reversal does not occur; tension deflated without resolution.
No reversal; the ongoing negative or positive condition intensifies.
No reversal; the scene establishes conditions for a later scene to exploit.
The existing balance holds despite pressure to change it.
Appears positive but contains the seed of a coming reversal.
Appears negative but signals the approach of reversal upward.
The protagonist achieves one value by surrendering another.
The protagonist wins the scene's external conflict but at unsustainable cost.
The protagonist loses materially but wins morally or internally.
The protagonist wins materially but at a moral or internal cost.
The scene ends with mixed valence: significant gain and significant loss simultaneously.

event_type.description: The grammatical type of event in a Canonical Summary scene turn — what kind of thing happened.

values.definition: A character performs a physical or behavioral action.
values.example_usage: 'Gregor crawled to the door.'

values.definition: A character makes a choice that shapes subsequent events.
values.example_usage: 'Elizabeth refused the offer.'

values.definition: New information is made available (to a character, the reader, or both).
values.example_usage: 'Grete discovered the furniture moving.'

values.definition: A significant verbal exchange between characters.
values.example_usage: 'Darcy declared his admiration.'

values.definition: A character's internal state changes — emotion, belief, resolve.
values.example_usage: 'Her contempt hardened to resolve.'

values.definition: The relationship between two characters changes.
values.example_usage: 'The family ceased addressing Gregor by name.'

values.definition: A character formulates or executes a plan.
values.example_usage: 'The lodgers devised a strategy to avoid Gregor.'

values.definition: A direct result or outcome of a prior event.
values.example_usage: 'The family's finances collapsed.'

values.definition: A force or entity impedes the character's want.
values.example_usage: 'The father blocked the doorway.'

values.definition: An unexpected development complicates the existing situation.
values.example_usage: 'The three lodgers threatened to break the lease.'

values.definition: An event in the story world not driven by a specific character's agency.
values.example_usage: 'The snow began to fall.'

want_outcome.description: The result of the focalizer's scene-level want at the end of the scene.

values.definition: The character fully achieved what they wanted in this scene.
The character achieved some but not all of their scene want.
The character did not achieve their scene want.
The want was not resolved; it carries over to subsequent scenes.
The character abandoned or transformed their want during the scene.
Events made the original want moot or impossible.

causal_role.description: The scene's causal position in the narrative chain — does it set up, follow from, or stand independently?

values.definition: This scene plants a cause whose effect will arrive in a later scene.
values.example_usage: A Chekhov's gun scene; an exposition scene that makes later events possible.

values.definition: This scene delivers the effect of a cause planted in an earlier scene.
values.example_usage: The gun fired; the promise kept or broken; the setup fulfilled.

values.definition: This scene introduces a factor that makes the existing situation more difficult.
values.example_usage: A new obstacle; an unexpected reversal; an escalation.

values.definition: This scene settles or closes an open question, conflict, or complication.
values.example_usage: A problem solved; a conflict ended; a decision made.

values.definition: This scene does not primarily function as setup or payoff for other scenes.
values.example_usage: A character study scene; a world-building scene.

values.definition: This scene occurs in parallel with events in another scene thread.
values.example_usage: Multi-POV narrative; B-thread scene.

stakes_domain.description: The category of value at risk in this scene.

values.definition: Physical safety, health, survival, or bodily integrity at risk.
values.example_usage: A fight; an illness; a dangerous journey.

values.definition: Mental health, emotional stability, sanity, or sense of self at risk.
values.example_usage: A psychological crisis; identity threat; emotional breakdown.

values.definition: Reputation, relationships, community standing, or social belonging at risk.
values.example_usage: A scandal; a breakup; social exile.

values.definition: The character's values, integrity, or ethical commitments are at risk.
values.example_usage: A temptation to compromise; a moral dilemma; a test of character.

values.definition: Money, property, economic security, or livelihood at risk.
values.example_usage: A bankruptcy; a business deal; a theft.

values.definition: Career, status, achievement, or professional identity at risk.
values.example_usage: A promotion; a dismissal; a professional test.

values.definition: A specific important relationship at risk.
values.example_usage: A friendship threatened; a marriage at a turning point; a family bond strained.

values.definition: Meaning, purpose, fundamental beliefs, or the character's sense of the world at risk.
values.example_usage: A crisis of faith; a philosophical reckoning; confrontation with mortality.
